summary: All four muscles of mastication are supplied by the mandibular division (V3) of the trigeminal nerve.
The muscles of mastication are supplied by the facial nerve (CN VII).
The muscles of mastication are supplied equally by V2 and V3 divisions.
The lateral pterygoid is the only muscle of mastication that depresses (opens) the mandible.
The medial pterygoid is the primary depressor of the mandible.
The maxillary artery is the terminal branch of the external carotid and is the dominant vessel of the infratemporal fossa.
The maxillary artery is a branch of the internal carotid artery.
The facial artery is the dominant vessel of the infratemporal fossa.
The middle meningeal artery enters the skull through the foramen spinosum.
The middle meningeal artery enters through the foramen ovale.
The middle meningeal artery enters through the foramen rotundum.
The pterygoid venous plexus communicates with the cavernous sinus, providing a route for intracranial spread of dental infections.
The pterygoid venous plexus drains directly into the internal jugular vein.
The pterygoid venous plexus has no intracranial communications.
In TMJ dislocation, the condyle slips anterior to the articular eminence and is locked by lateral pterygoid spasm.
In TMJ dislocation, the condyle slips posterior to the articular eminence.
TMJ dislocation involves the medial pterygoid muscle exclusively.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Revise the muscles and vascular relations.
Excellent understanding of the infratemporal region!
